perturbation.original_text: Distributor will comply with all country, federal, state and local laws, ordinances, codes, regulations, rules, policies and procedures, Including, without limitation, all anti-corruption laws, Including, the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (Applicable Laws). Distributor can find more information about the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act at the following URL: http://www.usdoj.gov/criminal/fraud/docs/dojdocb.html, o r b y contacting publicsectorcompllance@cisco.com.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor will endeavor to comply with country, federal, state and local laws, ordinances, codes, regulations, rules, policies and procedures, including anti-corruption laws. Distributor can find more information about the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act at the following URL: http://www.usdoj.gov/criminal/fraud/docs/dojdocb.html, o r b y contacting publicsectorcompllance@cisco.com.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will comply' to 'will endeavor to comply' introduces ambiguity. 'Endeavor' suggests an attempt, but not a guarantee of compliance, weakening the legal obligation. This creates uncertainty about the distributor's actual compliance requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FCPA requires strict compliance with anti-corruption laws. The modification weakens the commitment to comply, creating a loophole that could allow the distributor to claim they 'endeavored' to comply but ultimately failed, potentially violating the FCPA's mandate for full compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Criminal Division | Foreign Corrupt Practices Act Unit Skip to main content MENU Foreign Corrupt Practices Act Unit Corporate Enforcement Policy Statutes Legislative History FCPA Guidelines FCPA Resource Guide Enforcement Actions A - M N - Z Chronological List Corporate Enforcement Actions Individual Enforcement Actions FCPA Opinions Opinion Procedure Regulations (PDF) Opinion Releases Index Summaries Opinion Procedure Releases Review Procedure Releases International Agreements Anti-Corruption Sites Foreign Corrupt Practices Act Unit Share Facebook X LinkedIn Email An Overview Foreign Corrupt Practices Act The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act of 1977, as amended, 15 U.S.C. §§ 78dd-1, et seq. ("FCPA"), was enacted for the purpose of making it unlawful for certain classes of persons and entities to make payments to foreign government officials to assist in obtaining or retaining business. Specifically, the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA prohibit the willful use of the mails or any means of instrumentality of interstate commerce corruptly in furtherance of any offer, payment, promise to pay, or authorization of the payment of money or anything of value to any person, while knowing that all or a portion of such money or thing of value will be offered, given or promised, directly or indirectly, to a foreign official to influence the foreign official in his or her official capacity, induce the foreign official to do or omit to do an act in violation of his or her lawful duty, or to secure any improper advantage in order to assist in obtaining or retaining business for or with, or directing business to, any person. Since 1977, the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA have applied to all U.S. persons and certain foreign issuers of securities. With the enactment of certain amendments in 1998, the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA now also apply to foreign firms and persons who cause, directly or through agents, an act in furtherance of such a corrupt payment to take place within the territory of the United States. The FCPA also requires companies whose securities are listed in the United States to meet its accounting provisions. See 15 U.S.C. § 78m. These accounting provisions, which were designed to operate in tandem with the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA, require corporations covered by the provisions to (a) make and keep books and records that accurately and fairly reflect the transactions of the corporation and (b) devise and maintain an adequate system of internal accounting controls. perturbation.justification: The change from 'will comply' to 'will endeavor to comply' weakens the legal obligation to adhere to anti-corruption laws, including the FCPA. The original text ensures strict compliance, whereas the modified text introduces ambiguity by suggesting an attempt rather than a guarantee of compliance. The Law Explanation supports this by stating the FCPA requires strict compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that the FCPA was enacted 'for the purpose of making it unlawful for certain classes of persons and entities to make payments to foreign government officials to assist in obtaining or retaining business'. By weakening the compliance language, the changed text creates a loophole, potentially violating the FCPA's mandate, making the contradiction direct and impactful.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor shall not take any action or permit or authorize any action in violation of the Applicable Laws
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor shall, to the best of its understanding, not take any action or permit or authorize any action in violation of the Applicable Laws.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'to the best of its understanding' creates a loophole, where the distributor's subjective understanding determines compliance, instead of the objective laws. This makes the obligation unenforceable, as a distributor could claim a lack of understanding as a defense.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FCPA does not allow for subjective interpretations of compliance. It mandates adherence to the law regardless of the distributor's personal understanding. By introducing subjectivity, the modified text contradicts the FCPA's requirement for strict adherence to anti-corruption laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 78dd-2 - Prohibited foreign trade practices by domestic concerns | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 78dd-2 - Prohibited foreign trade practices by domestic concerns U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Prohibition It shall be unlawful for any domestic concern , other than an issuer which is subject to section 78dd–1 of this title , or for any officer, director, employee, or agent of such domestic concern or any stockholder thereof acting on behalf of such domestic concern, to make use of the mails or any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce corruptly in furtherance of an offer, payment, promise to pay, or authorization of the payment of any money, or offer, gift, promise to give, or authorization of the giving of anything of value to— (1) any foreign official for purposes of— (A) (i) influencing any act or decision of such foreign official in his official capacity, (ii) inducing such foreign official to do or omit to do any act in violation of the lawful duty of such official, or (iii) securing any improper advantage; or (B) inducing such foreign official to use his influence with a foreign government or instrumentality thereof to affect or influence any act or decision of such government or instrumentality, in order to assist such domestic concern in obtaining or retaining business for or with, or directing business to, any person; (2) any foreign political party or official thereof or any candidate for foreign political office for purposes of— (A) (i) influencing any act or decision of such party, official, or candidate in its or his official capacity, (ii) inducing such party, official, or candidate to do or omit to do an act in violation of the lawful duty of such party, official, or candidate, or (iii) securing any improper advantage; or (B) inducing such party, official, or candidate to use its or
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a subjective element ('to the best of its understanding') into the distributor's obligation to comply with applicable laws. This creates a loophole where the distributor's compliance is based on their subjective understanding rather than strict adherence to the law. The Law Explanation provided specifies that the FCPA mandates adherence to the law regardless of the distributor's personal understanding. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 U.S. Code § 78dd-2, outlines prohibited foreign trade practices and emphasizes the unlawful nature of certain actions, implying a standard of objective compliance rather than subjective interpretation. This directly contradicts the original intent, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined under 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' because it weakens the enforceability of the clause.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor remains responsible for undertaking appropriate and reasonable measures to ensure that its own relevant subcontractors, consultants, agents or representatives who interact with government​ affiliated organizations comply with applicable anti-corruption laws
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor will encourage its own relevant subcontractors, consultants, agents or representatives who interact with government affiliated organizations to comply with applicable anti-corruption laws.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'remains responsible for undertaking appropriate and reasonable measures to ensure that' with 'will encourage' significantly weakens the obligation. Encouragement is not a legally binding requirement and does not guarantee compliance by subcontractors. This removes the responsibility for ensuring compliance, potentially leading to violations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FCPA holds companies accountable for the actions of their agents. The modified text allows the distributor to avoid responsibility for subcontractors' violations by merely 'encouraging' compliance, directly conflicting with the FCPA's mandate for companies to actively prevent corruption by their agents and representatives.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: U.S.C. Title 15 - COMMERCE AND TRADE 15 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 15 - COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 2B - SECURITIES EXCHANGES Sec. 78dd-3 - Prohibited foreign trade practices by persons other than issuers or domestic concerns From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §78dd–3. Prohibited foreign trade practices by persons other than issuers or domestic concerns (a) Prohibition It shall be unlawful for any person other than an issuer that is subject to section 78dd–1 of this title or a domestic concern (as defined in section 78dd–2 of this title), or for any officer, director, employee, or agent of such person or any stockholder thereof acting on behalf of such person, while in the territory of the United States, corruptly to make use of the mails or any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or to do any other act in furtherance of an offer, payment, promise to pay, or authorization of the payment of any money, or offer, gift, promise to give, or authorization of the giving of anything of value to— (1) any foreign official for purposes of— (A)(i) influencing any act or decision of such foreign official in his official capacity, (ii) inducing such foreign official to do or omit to do any act in violation of the lawful duty of such official, or (iii) securing any improper advantage; or (B) inducing such foreign official to use his influence with a foreign government or instrumentality thereof to affect or influence any act or decision of such government or instrumentality, in order to assist such person in obtaining or retaining business for or with, or directing business to, any person; (2) any foreign political party or official thereof or any candidate for foreign political office for purposes of— (A)(i) influencing any act or decision of such party, official, or candidate in its or his official capacity, (ii) inducing such party, official, or candidate to do or omit to do an act in violation of the lawful duty of such party, official, or c
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation by replacing 'remains responsible for undertaking appropriate and reasonable measures to ensure that' with 'will encourage'. The law explanation states that the FCPA holds companies accountable for the actions of their agents, and the modified text allows the distributor to avoid responsibility for subcontractors' violations by merely 'encouraging' compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 1, U.S.C. Title 15 Sec. 78dd-3, prohibits certain foreign trade practices by persons other than issuers or domestic concerns, and includes agents of such persons. By only encouraging compliance, the distributor potentially fails to actively prevent corruption by its agents, contradicting the spirit and potentially the letter of the FCPA, as it implies a diminished responsibility for the actions of its subcontractors and representatives.
